system_prompt: You are participating in a research study about how AI models use and attribute information sources in AI-for-software-engineering questions. Answer carefully. Do not invent papers, URLs, DOIs, or venue facts. If uncertain, say so.
user_prompt:
Map the AI-for-software-engineering field into 6-8 active research areas. For each area, name the conferences where the work is most likely to appear and the kinds of sources a researcher should inspect first.

Return JSON with:
- ranked_items: list of objects with rank, area, conferences, source_types, justification
- references: list of source objects if available
- uncertainty_notes
response_text:
{
  "ranked_items": [
    {
      "rank": 1,
      "area": "Large Language Models for Code Generation and Completion",
      "conferences": ["ICSE", "FSE/ESEC", "ASE", "NeurIPS", "ICML", "ICLR", "ACL", "EMNLP", "PLDI"],
      "source_types": ["Conference papers (especially ICSE/FSE/ASE ML4Code tracks)", "arXiv preprints (moves very fast)", "Benchmark leaderboards (HumanEval, MBPP, CodeXGLUE, BigCodeBench)", "Model cards and technical reports from major labs (OpenAI, Anthropic, Meta, Google, BigCode)"],
      "justification": "This is currently the highest-velocity area. Foundational models (Codex, CodeLlama, StarCoder, DeepSeek-Coder, Qwen2.5-Coder) appear first on arXiv and lab blogs; evaluation methodology papers appear at ICSE/FSE/ASE and ML conferences. Benchmark saturation drives rapid iteration."
    },

      "conferences": ["ICSE", "FSE/ESEC", "ASE", "ISSTA", "ICST", "ICPC"],

      "justification": "Long-standing area revitalized by LLMs. Work appears at SE conferences with strong artifact evaluation. Benchmarks are critical for comparison; many papers now evaluate LLM-based repair against traditional approaches."
    },
